name: Strawman
definition: Misrepresenting an opponent’s argument (weaker version) and refuting that instead of the original.
why_it_weakens: It doesn’t address the real claim, so disagreement remains unresolved.
how_to_fix: Restate the strongest version of the other view (steelman) and confirm it before critiquing.
example: ‘So you’re saying we should never log anything’ (when the person argued for selective logging).

name: Red Herring
definition: Introducing an irrelevant point to distract from the issue under discussion.
why_it_weakens: It shifts the debate away from the key claim, preventing resolution.
how_to_fix: Name the tangent, park it, and return to the thesis question.
example: During an API design debate, switching to argue about team morale without linking it to the decision.

name: Ad Hominem
definition: Attacking the person (or their motives/traits) instead of the argument.
why_it_weakens: Personal attacks don’t change the truth of premises or the validity of inferences.
how_to_fix: Translate criticism into a claim about evidence, logic, or constraints.
example: ‘You always overcomplicate things’ instead of addressing the specific architectural tradeoff.

name: False Dilemma
definition: Presenting only two options when more exist, or when a spectrum/combination is possible.
why_it_weakens: It forces premature choices and hides better alternatives.
how_to_fix: Ask for the missing options; consider hybrids and phased approaches.
example: ‘Either we ship now or we do it perfectly’ (ignoring incremental delivery).

name: Slippery Slope
definition: Claiming that one step will inevitably lead to extreme consequences without sufficient support.
why_it_weakens: It substitutes fear of outcomes for evidence of causal links.
how_to_fix: Identify the mechanism and probability at each step; propose guardrails.
example: ‘If we add one endpoint, soon we’ll have a microservices mess.’

name: Post Hoc
definition: Assuming that because B followed A, A caused B.
how_to_fix: Look for confounders; propose an experiment or counterfactual check.
example: ‘We deployed and then latency rose, so the deploy caused it’ (ignoring traffic spike).

name: Circular Reasoning
definition: The conclusion is assumed in the premises (the argument goes in a circle).
why_it_weakens: It provides no independent support for the conclusion.
how_to_fix: Add an external premise that could be true or false independently of the conclusion.
example: ‘This design is best because it’s the best option.’

name: Equivocation
definition: Using a key term in multiple senses within the same argument without noticing.
why_it_weakens: The argument may appear valid while sliding between meanings.
how_to_fix: Define the term; replace it with two distinct terms if meanings differ.
example: Using ‘secure’ to mean ‘encrypted’ in one sentence and ‘authorized’ in the next.

name: Hasty Generalization
definition: Drawing a broad conclusion from too little or unrepresentative evidence.
why_it_weakens: The conclusion may not hold outside the small sample.
how_to_fix: Narrow the claim or gather more representative evidence.
example: ‘This approach failed once, so it never works.’

name: Appeal to Authority
definition: Claiming something is true primarily because an authority says so (especially outside their domain or without evidence).
why_it_weakens: Authorities can be wrong; truth still depends on reasons/evidence.
how_to_fix: Extract the underlying rationale; verify with evidence or tests.
example: ‘A famous engineer said microservices are best, so we should do it.’
